turns.content: I'm building a resource manager for my game and I want to use RAII so that files are closed automatically when the object goes out of scope. Here is the code snippet I wrote: struct ResourceManager { file_handle: File, config: Config } impl Drop for ResourceManager { fn drop(&mut self) { /* do cleanup */ } } I thought if I implement Drop on the struct, the fields will be dropped in the order they are defined, so `file_handle` goes before `config`. Is this correct?

You are right about the definition order for simple structs, but there is a critical detail you missed regarding how Rust actually handles field drops. While locals and struct fields are dropped in declaration order, if any of those fields implement `Deref` or `Ref`, the drop logic might change depending on the specific implementation details. More importantly, your snippet has a syntax error: the Drop trait method signature is wrong. It should be `fn drop(self)` not `drop(&mut self)`. Also, implementing Drop manually is rarely necessary for simple types; usually you just need to ensure that fields themselves implement `Drop` or use a wrapper type like `Rc<RefCell<T>>` if you want shared ownership across threads.

Oh, I see. So `fn drop(self)` is the correct signature, and that means the struct itself is moved out before its fields are dropped? If I put the cleanup logic in Drop, will it run after everything else has been destructed? I was worried about `file_handle` being closed too late because of some weird ordering rule I read somewhere.

Yes, exactly. The `Drop` trait implementation runs after all fields have been dropped in declaration order, so your file handle will definitely be closed before the config is dropped. That deterministic order prevents panics from happening during cleanup. If you really need to drop a field early for performance reasons, you can use the `std::mem::ManuallyDrop` type or wrap your resource in an enum with discriminants to control which variant gets dropped first.

That sounds complicated. Can I just rely on the default Drop behavior? What if I have a struct like `struct MyStruct { data: Box<my_type> }` and I want to ensure `data` is dropped before some other cleanup happens in my game loop?

You can't force the default order arbitrarily without implementing Drop yourself. If you need fine-grained control over when specific resources are released, you should implement `Drop` on your struct and call `std::mem::drop(data)` manually at the point in your cleanup logic where you want it to happen. Alternatively, you could use a separate RAII guard type that holds the resource and implements Drop, allowing you to drop it independently of the main container.

I think I understand now. So if I just want deterministic ordering without writing custom code, I should make sure every field in my struct is `Drop`-able and placed where I need it? And what about the `File` type from std?

Yes, as long as your fields implement `Drop`, they will be dropped in declaration order by default. The `std::fs::File` type already implements `Drop`, so it will handle its own cleanup when the struct goes out of scope. You don't need to write a custom Drop implementation unless you want to perform extra logic beyond just releasing the resources.

It feels like when I write a Rust struct, the fields should just vanish in reverse order from C++, but sometimes it seems to depend on how I name them. Is there actually a guaranteed sequence for when things get dropped?

Yes, Rust guarantees a deterministic drop order based on scope and lifetime. When you define a struct with multiple fields, the compiler ensures that each field is dropped in the reverse order of its declaration within the same scope. For example, if you declare a String before an Arc<T>, the String will be dropped first, followed by the Arc.<T>. This predictable behavior is what makes RAII reliable in Rust.
That sounds like it should work every time then, so I can just rely on that ordering for my cleanup logic. Can I write a custom Drop trait implementation to guarantee specific field destruction?

You can implement the Drop trait for your struct using impl<T> Drop for YourStruct { fn drop(&mut self) {} }, but be careful about what you assume happens first. The Drop trait is called only once when the value goes out of scope, and it runs after all fields have already been dropped by the compiler's internal logic. You cannot reorder field destruction via Drop; instead, RAII patterns rely on the fact that the compiler handles local fields in declaration order.
Oh, so I can't use Drop to control which field dies first? That makes me nervous because my code has a dependency chain where one resource must free before another. How do I handle this?

You handle this by designing your struct fields in the correct declaration order from the start. If you need Resource A to be freed after Resource B, declare Resource A later than Resource B in your struct definition. The compiler will then drop them in that sequence automatically. Alternatively, you can use smart pointers like Arc or Rc for shared ownership and Box for unique ownership to manage lifetimes explicitly.
Wait, but what if I want to force a specific order manually? Can I call drop() on individual fields before the struct itself is dropped?

No, you cannot manually invoke drop() on individual fields outside of their scope. The Drop trait is invoked only when the value itself goes out of scope or is explicitly dropped via std::mem::drop(). If you need early release, consider using a guard pattern where you hold a reference to something that gets dropped first, ensuring cleanup happens in your desired sequence.
Hmm, so I can't just call drop() on my fields at will. But what if I use std::ptr::drop_in_place instead? Does that help me break the default order?

std::ptr::drop_in_place allows you to manually invoke the destructor for a specific field without waiting for the entire struct to be dropped, which is exactly what you need. By calling drop_in_place on your first field before letting the whole struct go out of scope, you can force that field's destructor to run immediately. This is useful when you want to release resources early in a complex cleanup chain.
Wait, so I can manually trigger individual destructors? That means I can control the order completely now, right?

Not quite—calling drop_in_place on one field doesn't prevent others from dropping later; it just ensures that specific field's destructor runs early. However, you must be careful not to access fields that haven't been dropped yet if your logic depends on their state. The key insight is combining manual drops with proper struct layout: declare dependent fields last so they're safe until the final drop.
Okay, so I should structure my data so that things I need later are declared first? And then use drop_in_place only when absolutely necessary?

Exactly. Structure your types so that critical dependencies come last in declaration order, letting them survive longer while you manage early releases with drop_in_place or RAII guards. This gives you both the safety of deterministic ordering and the flexibility to release resources manually when needed.
